The asymptotic distribution presented in Theorem 6.2 is non-Gaussian. In the special case in which ∪ θ ∈ Θ δ ( P 0 ) Λ δ ( θ ; P 0 ) = { λ 12 } , the asymptotic distribution of ˆ κ δ simplifies to N (0 , λ ′ 12 Σ λ 12 ). An analogous simplification holds for ˆ κ δ when ∪ θ ∈ Θ δ ( P 0 ) Λ δ ( θ ; P 0 ) is a singleton.

## 6.2 Inference Procedure 1: Bootstrap

Our first inference procedure specializes the general approach of Fang and Santos (2019) for inference on directionally differentiable functions to the present setting. Define

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where

with ˆ ν a (possibly random) positive scalar tuning parameter for which ˆ ν → p ν &gt; 0. Any such ˆ ν results in a confidence set with asymptotically correct coverage. We give some practical guidance for choosing ˆ ν below.

Let ˆ P ∗ denote a bootstrapped version of ˆ P . In practice any bootstrap can be used provided it satisfies mild consistency conditions. In the empirical application in Section 5.1 we simply draw ˆ P ∗ ∼ N ( ˆ P, ˆ Σ /n ) where ˆ Σ is a consistent estimator of Σ. Let

where the quantiles are computed by resampling ˆ P ∗ (conditional on the data). Lower, upper, and two-sided 100(1 -α )% CSs for κ δ and κ δ are

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

We require a slight strengthening of Assumption M(vii) to establish validity of the procedure. As before, regularity conditions are presented for the general case where k depends on u . It may be possible to weaken these conditions when k does not depend on u .

Assumption M (continued) (vii') ( θ, P ) ↦→ Λ δ ( θ ; P ) and ( θ, P ) ↦→ Λ δ ( θ ; P ) are lower hemicontinuous at ( θ, P 0 ) for each θ ∈ Θ δ ( P 0 ) and θ ∈ Θ δ ( P 0 ) , respectively.

Theorem 6.3 Suppose that Assumptions F and M(i)-(vi),(vii') hold, √ n ( ˆ P -P 0 ) → d Z ∼ N (0 , Σ) with Σ finite, and ˆ P ∗ satisfies Assumption 3 of Fang and Santos (2019).

Then the distribution of ̂ db δ,P 0 [ √ n ( ˆ P ∗ -ˆ P )] and ̂ db δ,P 0 [ √ n ( ˆ P ∗ -ˆ P )] (conditional on the data) is consistent for the asymptotic distribution derived in Theorem 6.2. Moreover, if the CDFs of db δ,P 0 [ Z ] and db δ,P 0 [ Z ] are continuous and increasing at their α/ 2 , α , 1 -α , and 1 -α/ 2 quantiles, then

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Any ˆ ν that satisfies ˆ ν → p ν &gt; 0 results in asymptotically valid CSs. In view of the functional forms of ̂ db δ,P 0 [ · ] and ̂ db δ,P 0 [ · ], smaller ˆ ν produce (weakly) wider CSs. In the CSW application, we set ˆ ν equal to the minimum diagonal element of the covariance matrix of the moments evaluated at ( ˆ θ, ˆ γ, ˆ P ) under F ∗ , where ˆ θ is computed under F ∗ . We chose this quantity as it is related to the convexity of the inner problem for small δ . In practice, this resulted in ˆ ν between 0.001 and 0.01. We recommend setting ˆ ν to be of a similarly small magnitude, then performing a sensitivity analysis to check that critical values aren't too dependent on ˆ ν . Setting ˆ ν = 0 and replacing ˆ Θ δ,n and ˆ Θ δ,n by { ˆ θ δ } and { ˆ θ δ } where ˆ θ δ and ˆ θ δ minimize and maximize the sample criterions is also valid, but may be conservative.

If d 2 = 0, then ˆ c 1 -α, 1 is the (1 -α )-quantile of max 1 ≤ i ≤ d 1 Z i / ˆ σ i ; similarly, if d 1 = 0, then ˆ c 2 , 1 -α is the (1 -α )-quantile of max 1 ≤ i ≤ d 2 | Z i / ˆ σ i | .

## 7 Conclusion

This paper introduced a framework for analyzing the sensitivity of counterfactuals to parametric assumptions about the distribution of latent variables in structural models. In particular, we derived bounds on the set of counterfactuals obtained as the distribution of latent variables spans nonparametric neighborhoods of a given parametric specification while other 'structural' model features are maintained. We illustrated our procedure with empirical applications to matching models and dynamic discrete choice.

## References

- Aguirregabiria, V. and P. Mira (2007). Sequential estimation of dynamic discrete games. Econometrica 75 (1), 1-53.
- Allen, R. and J. Rehbeck (2019). Identification with additively separable heterogeneity. Econometrica 87 (3), 1021-1054.
- Andrews, I., M. Gentzkow, and J. M. Shapiro (2017). Measuring the sensitivity of parameter estimates to estimation moments. The Quarterly Journal of Economics 132 (4), 1553-1592.
- Andrews, I., M. Gentzkow, and J. M. Shapiro (2020). On the informativeness of descriptive statistics for structural estimates. Econometrica (6), 2231-2258.
- Armstrong, T. B. and M. Koles´ ar (2021). Sensitivity analysis using approximate moment condition models. Quantitative Economics 12 (1), 77-108.
- Bajari, P., C. L. Benkard, and J. Levin (2007). Estimating dynamic models of imperfect competition. Econometrica 75 (5), 1331-1370.
- Beresteanu, A., I. Molchanov, and F. Molinari (2011). Sharp identification regions in models with convex moment predictions. Econometrica 79 (6), 1785-1821.
- Berger, J. O. (1984). The robust Bayesian viewpoint (with discussion). In J. B. Kadane (Ed.), Robustness of Bayesian analyses . North-Holland.
- Berry, S. T. (1992). Estimation of a model of entry in the airline industry. Econometrica 60 (4), 889-917.
- Berry, S. T. and P. A. Haile (2010). Nonparametric identification of multinomial choice demand models with heterogeneous consumers. Cowles Foundation Discussion Paper No. 1718.

## Online Appendix to 'Counterfactual Sensitivity and Robustness'

## Timothy Christensen

Benjamin Connault

This supplement presents extensions of our methodology in Appendix A, additional results on nonparametric bounds on counterfactuals in Appendix B, connections with local approaches to sensitivity analysis in Appendix C, additional details on the empirical applications in Appendix D, and proofs of results from the main text in Appendix E.

## A Extensions

This appendix presents three extensions of our methodology. Proofs of all results in this appendix are deferred to Appendix G.7.

## A.1 Group Invariance

In certain settings it can be attractive to impose shape restrictions on F such as symmetry, exchangeability, or, more generally, invariance to a finite group of transforms. For instance, imposing exchangeability of F in discrete choice modeling ensures that alternatives' choice probabilities depend on their deterministic components of utility but not their labeling. These shape restrictions can be easily imposed whenever F ∗ is invariant.

Formally, let J denote the number of elements of U and let Π be a finite commutative group of transforms on R J -see, e.g., Section 1.4 of Lehmann and Casella (1998). We say that a distribution F of U is Π -invariant if glyph[pi1] U ∼ F for all glyph[pi1] ∈ Π.

Example A.1 (Symmetry) Central symmetry corresponds to Π = { I, -I } for I the identity matrix. Sign symmetry corresponds to taking Π to be the collection of all 2 J diagonal matrices with ± 1 in each diagonal entry. glyph[square]

Example A.2 (Exchangeability) Let Π J denote the group of all J ! permutation matrices of dimension J . Full exchangeability (permutation invariance) corresponds to Π = Π J . Cyclic exchangeability (rotation invariance) corresponds to Π = Π c J where Π c J is the collection of all J cyclic permutation matrices of dimension J (Π c J = Π J when J = 2 and is a strict subset otherwise). When J ≥ 3, dihedral exchangeability (rotation and reflection invariance) corresponds to taking Π to be the set of all 2 J

glyph[negationslash]

permutation matrices representing rotations and reflections of { 1 , . . . , J } . These types of exchangeability ensure the elements of U are identically distributed, but they have different implications for the joint distribution of the elements of U . For instance, the distribution of ( U i , U j ) for i = j depends on i -j and | i -j | under cyclic and dihedral exchangeability, but is independent of ( i, j ) under full exchangeability. glyph[square]

Let N Π δ = { F ∈ N δ : F is Π-invariant } . We are interested in

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

and κ Π δ defined as the analogous supremum. One may write κ Π δ and κ Π δ as the value of two optimization problems in which criterion functions K Π δ ( θ ; γ 0 , P 0 ) and K Π δ ( θ ; γ 0 , P 0 ) are optimized with respect to θ . For a generic ( θ, γ, P ), define

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

and define K Π δ ( θ ; γ, P ) as the analogous supremum. These criterions have dual representations as finite-dimensional convex programs when F ∗ is Π-invariant. Define

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where | Π | denotes the cardinality of Π, and let g Π = ( g Π 1 , g Π 2 , g Π 3 , g Π 4 ).

Proposition A.1 Suppose that Assumption F holds and F ∗ is Π -invariant. Then

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Moreover, the value of problem (34) is + ∞ (equivalently, the value of problem (35) is -∞ ) if and only if there is no distribution in N Π δ under which (1) holds at ( θ, γ, P ) .

Remark A.1 If F is Π -invariant and satisfies (1), then it must also satisfy (1) under all | Π | transformations of the elements of U . Therefore, in effect there are a total of

| Π | × d moment conditions imposed in the inner optimization, namely

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

In principle one could form a criterion by including all | Π |× d moments. By Π -invariance of F ∗ and convexity of the objective, the multipliers on the moments g ( glyph[pi1] U, θ, γ ) will be identical across all glyph[pi1] ∈ Π . It therefore suffices to form the criterion using only the d averaged moments g Π rather than the full set of | Π | × d moments, thereby reducing the dimension of the inner optimization by a factor of | Π | .

Remark A.2 When Monte Carlo integration is used to compute expectations, taking a sample from F ∗ and then concatenating the sample across each of its | Π | transformations ensures the empirical distribution of the random draws is Π -invariant.

Suppose the researcher assumes U | X = x ∼ F ∗ for each x . We wish to relax this assumption and allow each conditional distribution of U given X = x , say F x , to vary in a neighborhood N δ x of F ∗ . In doing so, we are allowing the conditional distributions F x to vary with x , and therefore relaxing independence of U and X . 25

## C Local Sensitivity

In this appendix, we first introduce a measure of local sensitivity of the counterfactual with respect to F . We then contrast our approach with recent work on local sensitivity.

## C.1 Measure of Local Sensitivity

Our measure of local sensitivity of the counterfactual κ with respect to F at F ∗ is

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

If s is finite, then under the regularity conditions below

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where κ ∗ = E F ∗ [ k ( U, θ ∗ , γ 0 )] and θ ∗ solves (1) under F ∗ .

To draw connections with the local sensitivity literature, we restrict attention to moment equality models and impose (standard) regularity conditions. These conditions allow us to characterize s very tractably via an influence function representation, which leads to a simple estimator ˆ s of s . Assume that under F ∗ the moment conditions (1b) and (1d) point identify a structural parameter θ ∗ ∈ int(Θ), where we again assume Θ is compact. With some abuse of notation, let

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

g ∗ ( u ) = g ( u, θ ∗ , γ 0 , P 20 ), and k ∗ ( u ) = k ( u, θ ∗ , γ 0 ). Let E F ∗ [ g ( U, θ, γ 0 , P 20 )] and E F ∗ [ k ( U, θ, γ 0 )] be continuously differentiable with respect to θ at θ ∗ , G = ∂ ∂θ ′ E F ∗ [ g ( U, θ, γ 0 , P 20 )] ∣ ∣ θ = θ ∗ have full rank, V = E F ∗ [ g ∗ ( U ) g ∗ ( U ) ′ ] be finite and positive definite, E F ∗ [ k ( U, θ ∗ , γ 0 ) 2 ] be finite, and k ( · , θ, γ 0 ) and g ( · , θ, γ 0 , P 20 ) be L 2 ( F ∗ )-continuous in θ at θ ∗ .

Define the influence function of the counterfactual κ with respect to F at F ∗ as

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where M k ∗ ( u ) = k ∗ ( u ) -κ ∗ -E F ∗ [ k ∗ ( U ) g ∗ ( U ) ′ ]( V -1 -V -1 G ( G ′ V -1 G ) -1 G ′ V -1 ) g ∗ ( u ) and J = ∂ ∂θ E F ∗ [ k ( U, θ, γ 0 )] ∣ ∣ θ = θ ∗ . The following theorem relates s and ι . We restrict attention to neighborhoods characterized by χ 2 divergence. Other φ -divergences are locally equivalent to χ 2 divergence, so this restriction entails no great loss of generality. 26

Theorem C.1 Suppose that the above GMM-type regularity conditions hold and neighborhoods are defined using χ 2 divergence. Then s = 2 E F ∗ [ ι ( U ) 2 ] .

The proof of Theorem C.1 is presented in Appendix G.9. In addition to reporting an estimated counterfactual ˆ κ = E F ∗ [ k ( U, ˆ θ, ˆ γ )], researchers could also report an estimate of its local sensitivity to F :

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where ˆ k ( u ) = k ( u, ˆ θ, ˆ γ ), ˆ g ( u ) = g ( u, ˆ θ, ˆ γ, ˆ P 2 ), ˆ V = E F ∗ [ˆ g ( U )ˆ g ( U ) ′ ], and

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

with ˆ G = ∂ ∂θ ′ E F ∗ [ g ( U, θ, ˆ γ, ˆ P 2 )] | θ = ˆ θ and ˆ J = ∂ ∂θ E F ∗ [ k ( U, θ, ˆ γ )] | θ = ˆ θ . Lemma G.12 in Appendix G.9 shows ˆ s is consistent. Bounds on counterfactuals as F varies over small neighborhoods of F ∗ can then be estimated using ˆ κ ± √ δ ˆ s .

## C.2 Comparison with Other Approaches

We now compare our approach with Andrews et al. (2017, 2020), henceforth AGS, and Bonhomme and Weidner (2021), henceforth BW. To simplify the comparison, we consider models characterized by moments of the form (1b) with d 2 ≥ d θ and in which there is no γ .

26 See Theorem 4.1 of Csisz´ ar and Shields (2004). The quantity 2 E F ∗ [ ι ( U ) 2 ] should be rescaled by a factor of φ ′′ (1) for other φ divergences.

AGS consider a setting in which the moments (1b) are locally misspecified:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Suppose a researcher has a first-stage estimator ˆ P 2 , computes an estimator ˆ θ by minimizing

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

then estimates the counterfactual using ˆ κ = E F ∗ [ k ( U, ˆ θ )]. AGS's measure of sensitivity of ˆ κ to ˆ P 2 is J ′ ( G ′ WG ) -1 G ′ W , where W is the probability limit of ˆ W . The first-order asymptotic bias of ˆ κ due to local misspecification is therefore J ′ ( G ′ WG ) -1 G ′ Wc . AGS's measure of informativeness of ˆ P 2 for ˆ κ is 1, meaning that all sampling variation in ˆ κ is explained by sampling variation in ˆ P 2 . Our measure s instead characterizes 'specification variation' in κ as the researcher varies F subject to the moment condition (1b).

BW consider estimation of a target parameter ( κ in our context) using a reference model M R = { ( θ, F ) ∈ Θ ×{ F ∗ }} when the true ( θ 0 , F 0 ) possibly belongs to a larger model M L = { ( θ, F ) ∈ Θ × N δ } with δ ↓ 0 as the sample size n increases so that nδ → τ ≥ 0. BW seek estimators of κ under M R that minimize worst-case asymptotic bias or MSE over M L . Consider the one-step estimator

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where ˆ θ is a √ n -consistent estimator of θ ∗ and a ∈ R d 2 satisfies J ′ = -a ′ G so that ˆ κ does not depend asymptotically on ˆ θ . The true counterfactual is κ 0 = E F 0 [ k ( U, θ 0 )] where ( θ 0 , F 0 ) ∈ M L satisfies E F 0 [ g 2 ( U, θ 0 )] = P 20 . If M R is correctly specified so that E F ∗ [ g 2 ( U, θ ∗ )] = P 20 , then for any a the worst-case asymptotic bias of the one-step estimator is

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where s is our measure of local sensitivity.

If we allow for local misspecification of M R , so that E F ∗ [ g 2 ( U, θ ∗ )] = P 20 , then the worst-case asymptotic bias of the one-step estimator is glyph[negationslash]

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where s a is our local sensitivity measure with k replaced by k + a ′ g 2 .

Figure 5: Fixedθ bounds on the 'some college' to 'college graduate' premium when structural parameters are held fixed at CSW's estimates.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

## D Additional Details for the Empirical Applications

## D.1 Marital College Premium

Bootstrap Details. CSs reported Section 5.1 with δ &gt; 0 are computed using the bootstrap procedure from Section 6.2. To implement the bootstrap, we take 1,000 independent draws of ˆ P ∗ 2 ∼ N ( ˆ P 2 , ˆ Σ) where ˆ Σ is CSW's estimate of the covariance matrix of ˆ P 2 . We compute ˆ P 2 and ˆ Σ based on CSW's replication files.

Fixedθ Bounds. Figure 5a plots lower and upper bounds on the 'some college' to 'college graduate' premium across cohorts when θ is fixed at CSW's estimates (computed under F ∗ ) but F is allowed to vary. These bounds for large δ contain zero across each cohort, and are approximately the same width as the bounds with δ = 0 . 01 reported in Figure 1a where both θ and F are allowed to vary. Imposing exchangeability (Figure 5b) is seen to tighten the bounds substantially, producing bounds that span negative values only for early cohorts and positive values only in the latest few cohorts.

Projection CSs. Figure 6 reports projection CSs computed using the procedure in Section 6.3. We formed 95% rectangular CSs for each cohort's P 20 as described in Section 6.3 using CSW's estimates for ˆ P 2 and their asymptotic variance estimates for ˆ Σ. These CSs are significantly wider than the bootstrap CSs reported in Figure 1. Some conservativeness is to be expected, as these CSs project a 95% CS for P 20 down to one dimension. The relative inefficiency is especially pronounced for the earlier cohorts. Note also from Figure 6b that the projection CSs with δ = 0 . 01 span zero across each cohort,

Figure 6: Projection 95% CSs for bounds on the 'some college' to 'college graduate' premium across cohorts.

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Table 5: Computation times for the inner problem in the matching application

| Implementation          |     δ |     δ |     δ |      δ |       δ |
|-------------------------|-------|-------|-------|--------|---------|
|                         | 0.01  | 0.1   | 1     | 10     | 100     |
| Without exchangeability | 0.074 | 0.056 | 0.076 |  0.579 |   0.188 |
| With exchangeability    | 0.146 | 0.184 | 0.35  |  0.311 |   0.488 |

Note: Times (in seconds) for solving the inner optimization problem for maximizing the premium in cohort 1 at CSW's parameter estimate θ . We use 50,000 Monte Carlo draws without exchangeability and 120,000 draws with exchangeability. All computations are performed in Julia version 1.6.4 and Knitro 12.4.0 on a 2.7GHz MacBook Pro with 16GB memory.

whereas the bootstrap CSs with δ = 0 . 01 in Figure 1b contain negative values only in some early cohorts and positive values only in later cohorts.

Computation Times. Table 5 reports times for solving the inner problem for maximizing the premium in cohort 1. This optimization problem defines the criterion function K δ ( θ ; ˆ P ). As times vary with θ , we report times at CSW's estimates. Times increase somewhat with δ , but are all under 0.6 seconds. The outer optimization times varied with cohort, δ , and implementation but were typically solved in at most a few minutes (often under 90 seconds).

Sensitivity to φ . Using χ 2 and L 4 divergences produced near identical bounds for δ = 0 . 01 and 0 . 1. The χ 2 bounds with δ = 1 and 10 were at most 10% narrower than the hybrid bounds. The L 4 bounds were 60%-70% of the width of the hybrid bounds for δ = 1, 10, and 100 across cohorts ( L 4 divergence is stronger than χ 2 and hybrid divergence). The shapes of the sets were also similar to those reported for hybrid

divergence. Overall, these results show that the conclusions we draw from our analysis are not sensitive to the choice of φ .

## D.2 Welfare Analysis in a Rust Model

Bootstrap Details. Bootstrap CSs reported Section 5.2 with δ &gt; 0 are computed using the procedure from Section 6.2. We take 1,000 independent draws of ˆ θ ∗ π ∼ N ( ˆ θ π , ˆ Σ) where ˆ θ π is the MLE of ( RC,MC ) under the i.i.d. Gumbel assumption and ˆ Σ is an estimate of the inverse information matrix. We then set ˆ P ∗ 2 to be the model-implied CCPs at ˆ θ ∗ π under the i.i.d. Gumbel assumption.

As k depends only implicitly on u through θ , we compute ˆ κ δ and ˆ κ δ using the criterion functions in display (17), which is more computationally efficient than using criterions (13) and (14). The λ multipliers on the minimum divergence problem ∆( θ ; P ) in (17) differ from λ in criterions (13) and (14) by the factor η (see the discussion in Section 2.3). As our bootstrap methods are derived based on criterions (13) and (14), when implementing the bootstrap we rescale the multiplier λ solving (16) by the multiplier η on the constraint ∆( θ ; ˆ P ) ≤ δ in the outer optimization. 27 As η and λ are computed separately in the outer and inner optimizations, respectively, it is computationally most convenient to implement our bootstrap CSs with ˆ ν = 0. As discussed in Section 6.2, this choice is valid but possibly conservative. Despite this potentially conservative choice, the bootstrap CSs are not materially wider than the bootstrap CSs under the i.i.d. Gumbel assumption.

To construct the projection CSs, we form a 95% rectangular CS for P 20 as described in Section 6.3. For each draw of ˆ θ ∗ π we compute the model-implied CCPs ˆ P ∗ 2 under the i.i.d. Gumbel assumption. We construct t -statistics for each CCP by centering ˆ P ∗ 2 at ˆ P 2 and studentizing by its standard deviation across draws. For each draw we compute the maximum of the absolute value of the t -statistics. We then take the critical value ˆ c 2 , 1 -α to be the 1 -α quantile of the maximum statistic across draws.

27 This rescaling is also justified as follows. Let b δ ( P ) = sup θ ∈ Θ:∆( θ ; P ) ≤ δ k ( θ ) and note κ δ = b δ ( P 0 ) and ˆ κ δ = b δ ( ˆ P ). By similar arguments to Corollary 5 of Milgrom and Segal (2002), one may deduce that the directional derivative of b δ ( P ) at P 0 involves multiplying the directional derivative of P ↦→ ∆( θ ; P ) at P 0 by the multiplier for ∆( θ ; P ) ≤ δ . The directional derivative of P ↦→ ∆( θ ; P ) at P 0 may be shown to be

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where Λ( θ ; P 0 ) is constructed analogously to Λ δ ( θ ; P ) in Section 6.2 using the set of multipliers that solve the minimum divergence problem (16).

Table 6: Computation times for the inner problem in the DDC application

|             |     δ |     δ |     δ |      δ |       δ |
|-------------|-------|-------|-------|--------|---------|
|             | 0.01  | 0.1   | 1     | 10     | 100     |
| Lower bound | 0.124 | 0.144 | 0.164 |  0.285 |   0.265 |
| Upper bound | 0.101 | 0.119 | 0.142 |  0.266 |   1.039 |

Note: Times (in seconds) for solving the inner optimization problem at the parameter values at which ˆ κ δ and ˆ κ δ are attained. All computations are performed in Julia version 1.6.4 and Knitro 12.4.0 on a 2.7GHz MacBook Pro with 16GB memory.

Computation times. Table 6 reports computation times for the inner optimization for evaluating the criterion functions ˆ K δ ( θ ; ˆ γ, ˆ P ) and ˆ K δ ( θ ; ˆ γ, ˆ P ) at the parameter values at which ˆ κ δ and ˆ κ δ are attained. The computation times correspond to solving the minimum divergence problem ∆( θ ; ˆ γ, ˆ P ) because k does not depend on u (cf. display (17)). The outer optimizations were typically solved in a few minutes in a 8-core environment with 64GB memory.

Sensitivity to φ . Bounds with χ 2 -divergence were between 4% narrower and 1% wider than the bounds for hybrid divergence for all values of δ . Repeating the analysis with L 4 -divergence, which is stronger than χ 2 and hybrid divergence, produced bounds that were 10-30% narrower than the hybrid divergence bounds up to δ = 1 and at most 5% narrower than the hybrid divergence bounds for larger values of δ . As with the matching application, these results again show that the conclusions we draw from our analysis are not sensitive to the choice of φ function.

## E Proofs of Main Results

Throughout the proofs, we abbreviate upper-semicontinuous and upper-semicontinuity to u.s.c. and lower-semicontinuous and lower-semicontinuity to l.s.c.

## E.1 Proofs for Section 2

Proof of Proposition 2.1. Immediate from Proposition G.1 in Appendix G.2.

Recall Condition S from Definition 2.1 and Condition S np from Definition B.1.

Lemma E.1 Suppose that Assumption F holds and µ and F ∗ are mutually absolutely continuous. Then Condition S holds at ( θ, γ, P ) if and only if Condition S np holds at ( θ, γ, P ) .

Proof of Lemma E.1. In view of H¨ older's inequality for Orlicz classes (see (A.1)), Assumption F implies N ∞ = { F : D φ ( F ‖ F ∗ ) &lt; ∞} ⊆ F θ . Therefore,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

By Corollary 6.6.2 of Rockafellar (1970), it suffices to show ri( G ∞ ) = ri( G θ ). As ri( G ∞ ) ⊆ G ∞ ⊆ G θ , it suffices to show G θ ⊆ cl( G ∞ ) (Hiriart-Urruty and Lemar´ echal, 2001, Remark 2.1.9). For any x ∈ G θ , we have x = E F [ g ( U, θ, γ )] for some F ∈ F θ . As F glyph[lessmuch] µ and F ∗ and µ are mutually absolutely continuous, F has a density, say m , with respect to F ∗ . For each n ≥ 1, let m ( u ) ∧ n = min { m ( u ) , n } and define

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Each F n defined by d F n = m n d F ∗ belongs to N ∞ . It follows that E F n [ g ( U, θ, γ )] ∈ G ∞ . By monotone convergence, we have E F n [ g ( U, θ, γ )] → x . Therefore, x ∈ cl( G ∞ ). Proof of Theorem 2.1. We prove only the result for inf K ; the result for sup K follows similarly. Note

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the first equality is by definition and the second equality holds because, if θ glyph[negationslash]∈ Θ I , then there does not exist a distribution F ∈ F θ under which the moment conditions hold at ( θ, γ 0 , P 0 ) and consequently K np ( θ ; γ 0 , P 0 ) = + ∞ . If θ glyph[negationslash]∈ Θ I , then there does not exist F ∈ N ∞ under which the moment conditions hold at ( θ, γ 0 , P 0 ) either because N ∞ ⊆ F θ for all θ under Assumption F . Therefore, K ∞ ( θ ; γ 0 , P 0 ) = + ∞ in that case too. We therefore have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

In view of Lemma B.1, it suffices to show inf K = inf K ∞ . Note that inf K ≤ inf K ∞ holds by virtue of the inclusion N ∞ ⊆ F θ for all θ . For the reverse inequality, choose any glyph[epsilon1] &gt; 0. By S -regularity of Θ I , there exists θ ∈ Θ I for which Condition S holds at ( θ, γ 0 , P 0 ) and for which K np ( θ ; γ 0 , P 0 ) ≤ inf K + glyph[epsilon1] . As Condition S holds at ( θ, γ 0 , P 0 ) and µ glyph[lessmuch] F ∗ glyph[lessmuch] µ , Lemma E.1 implies that Condition S np must also hold at ( θ, γ 0 , P 0 ). Moreover, the µ -essential infimum and F ∗ -essential infimum of any function are equal because µ glyph[lessmuch] F ∗ glyph[lessmuch] µ . Therefore by Lemmas B.2 and B.3, we have K ∞ ( θ ; γ 0 , P 0 ) = K np ( θ ; γ 0 , P 0 ). It follows by definition of inf K ∞ that inf K ∞ ≤ K ∞ ( θ ; γ 0 , P 0 ) = K np ( θ ; γ 0 , P 0 ) ≤ inf K + glyph[epsilon1] . Therefore, inf K ∞ ≤ inf K .

and v B = + ∞ if there is no solution to this problem. Program A is the approach described in Section 2 whereas Program B is equivalent to our MPEC implementation.

The inequality v A ≤ v B is trivial if v B = + ∞ . If v B is finite, for any ε &gt; 0 there exists θ B ε ∈ Θ for which E F δ,θ B ε [ k ( U, θ B ε , γ )] ≤ v B + ε and E F δ,θ B ε [ g 4 e ( U, θ B ε , γ )] = 0 where F δ,θ ε B is well defined by Lemma G.2(ii). As ( θ B ε , F δ,θ ε B ) are feasible for Program A, we have v A ≤ v B + ε . As ε is arbitrary, we have v A ≤ v B whenever v B &gt; -∞ .

A similar argument applies when v B = -∞ : for any n ∈ N there exists θ B n ∈ Θ for which E F δ,θ B n [ k ( U, θ B ε , γ )] ≤ -n and E F δ,θ B n [ g 4 e ( U, θ B ε , γ )] = 0, where the distribution F δ,θ B n is well defined by Lemma G.2(ii). As ( θ B n , F δ,θ B n ) are feasible for Program A, we have v A ≤ -n . Hence, v A = v B = -∞ .

Note v B ≤ v A holds trivially if v A = + ∞ . If v A is finite, rewrite Program B as

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where F δ,θ,κ solves the feasibility program

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

For any ε &gt; 0 there exists θ A ε ∈ Θ and F A ε ∈ N δ such that the constraints in Program A are satisfied, i.e. E F ε A [ g 1 ( U, θ A ε , γ )] ≤ P 1 , . . . , E F ε A [ g 4 ( U, θ A ε , γ )] = 0, and

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Then F A ε solves the feasibility program (49) with θ = θ A ε and κ = κ A ε := E F ε A [ k ( U, θ A ε , γ )].

Note that E F ε A [ g 4 e ( U, θ A ε , γ )] = 0 also holds by construction. Therefore, ( κ A ε , θ A ε ) are feasible for the augmented form of Program B. It follows that v B ≤ κ A ε ≤ v A + ε holds for each ε &gt; 0. As ε &gt; 0 is arbitrary, we have v B ≤ v A whenever v A &gt; -∞ .

A similar argument applies if v A = -∞ : for any n ∈ N , we may choose θ A n ∈ Θ and F A n ∈ N δ such that the constraints in Program A are satisfied and E F A n [ k ( U, θ A n , γ )] ≤ -n . It follows that v B ≤ -n . Hence, v B = v A = -∞ .

Proof of Proposition 3.2. We prove the result for F δ,θ , the result for F δ,θ follows similarly. We drop dependence of k and g on ( θ, γ ) to simplify notation in what follows.

First, suppose k depends on u . The dual formulation is justified by Proposition 2.1. A dual solution ( η, ζ, λ ) exists by Proposition G.1(iii).

Suppose η &gt; 0. We wish to show that the change of measure m δ,θ ( u ) = ˙ φ glyph[star] ( -η -1 ( k ( u )+ ζ + λ ′ g s ( u ))) induces a distribution that solves the primal problem (20) at θ . which implies E F ∗ [ m δ,θ ] = 1 and hence that F δ,θ is a probability measure. The FOC for λ is

hence (1a)-(1c) and E F [ g 4 s ( U, θ, γ )] = 0 hold at ( θ, γ, P ) under F δ,θ . The FOC for η &gt; 0 is

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for any x glyph[star] in the range of ˙ φ glyph[star] (Rockafellar, 1970, Theorem 26.4). Setting x glyph[star] = ˙ φ glyph[star] ( x ) and noting that φ glyph[star]glyph[star] = φ holds by the Fenchel-Moreau theorem, we obtain

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

It follows that we may rewrite the FOC for η as δ = E F ∗ [ φ ( m δ,θ ( U )) ] and so F δ,θ ∈ N δ . Now suppose η = 0. Here we wish to show that m δ,θ ( u ) = 1l { u ∈ A δ,θ } /F ∗ ( A δ,θ ) induces a distribution that solves the primal problem (20) at θ . As the neighborhood constraint F ∈ N δ is not binding, the value of the objective must be the same as the optimal value when δ = ∞ . In view of Lemma B.2, the value is F ∗ -ess inf( k ( · )+ λ ′ g s ( · )) -λ ′ 12 P . We can write problem (22) as a nested optimization:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

At λ = λ , the inner problem is the dual of inf F ∈N δ E F [ k ( U ) + λ ′ g s ( U ) -λ ′ 12 P ]. As η = 0, the constraint F ∈ N δ is not binding and so the minimizing distribution must be supported on A δ,θ . Finally, by convexity of φ , the distribution induced by m δ,θ minimizes D φ ( · ‖ F ∗ ) among all distributions with support A δ,θ .

Now suppose k does not depend on u . By Proposition G.2, the primal and dual values of (15) are equal and a dual solution exists. By similar arguments to above, E F ∗ [ m δ,θ ( U )] = 1, and (1a)-(1c) and E F [ g 4 s ( U, θ, γ )] = 0 hold at ( θ, γ, P ) under F δ,θ . Finally, as there exists F ∈ N δ under which the moment conditions (1a)-(1c) and E F [ g 4 s ( U, θ, γ )] = 0 hold at ( θ, γ, P ), we must have D ( F δ,θ ‖ F ∗ ) ≤ D ( F ‖ F ∗ ) ≤ δ , as required.

## E.3 Proofs for Section 4

Proof of Proposition 4.1. As φ 1 ( x ) ≤ ¯ aφ 2 ( x ) for all x &gt; 0, we have D φ 1 ( F ‖ F ∗ ) ≤ ¯ aD φ 2 ( F ‖ F ∗ ). Hence, N δ, 2 ⊆ N ¯ aδ, 1 for each δ &gt; 0. The result follows from this inclusion, noting that κ ¯ aδ, 1 and κ ¯ aδ, 1 are both finite because Assumption F holds for φ 1 .

Lemma E.5 Suppose that Assumptions F and M(i),(ii) hold, Condition S' holds at ( θ, γ, P ) , and ∆( θ ; γ, P ) &lt; δ . Then there is a neighborhood N of ( θ, γ, P ) such that Condition S' holds at ( ˜ θ, ˜ γ, ˜ P ) and ∆( ˜ θ ; ˜ γ, ˜ P ) &lt; δ for all ( ˜ θ, ˜ γ, ˜ P ) ∈ N .

Proof of Lemma E.5. By Lemma G.7, Condition S' holds at all ( ˜ θ, ˜ γ, ˜ P ) in a neighborhood N ′ of ( θ, γ, P ). Moreover, ∆( ˜ θ ; ˜ γ, ˜ P ) &lt; δ holds at all ( ˜ θ, ˜ γ, ˜ P ) in a neighborhood N ′′ of ( θ, γ, P ) by Lemma G.8. Set N = N ′ ∩ N ′′ .

In the remainder of this subsection we drop dependence of all quantities on γ .

Step 3: In view of step 2, the asymptotic distribution follows by Theorem 2.1 of Shapiro (1991) and the fact that √ n ( ˆ P -P ) → d N (0 , Σ).

Proof of Theorem 6.3. We verify the conditions of Theorem 3.2 of Fang and Santos (2019). Their Assumptions 1 and 2 hold by Theorem 6.2 and because √ n ( ˆ P -P 0 ) → d N (0 , Σ) with Σ finite, respectively. Their Assumption 3 is assumed directly. Finally, Lemma G.11 shows that ̂ db δ,P 0 and ̂ db δ,P 0 satisfy the sufficient conditions for Assumption

4 of Fang and Santos (2019), which is presented in their Remark 3.4. This proves consistency. Coverage of CS 1 -α δ,L and CS 1 -α δ,U follows by continuity of the distribution functions. Coverage of CS 1 -α δ follows by the Bonferroni inequality.

Proof of Theorem 6.4. We prove the result only for CS 1 -α δ ; the result for the other CSs follow similarly. Say that P 0 ∈ CS 1 -α P 0 if P 10 ≤ ˆ P 1 -α 1 ,U and P 20 ∈ [ ˆ P 1 -α 2 ,L , ˆ P 1 -α 2 ,U ] both hold. By Lemma E.3, for each ε &gt; 0 we may choose θ ε , θ ε ∈ Θ δ ( P 0 ) such that K δ ( θ ε ; P 0 ) &lt; κ δ + ε and K δ ( θ ε ; P 0 ) &gt; κ δ -ε . Let F θ ε and F θ ε solve problem (15) at ( θ ε ; P 0 ) and ( θ ε ; P 0 ), respectively. Whenever P 0 ∈ CS 1 -α P 0 holds, F θ ε and F θ ε must also satisfy the 'relaxed' moment conditions used for computing ˆ κ δ, 1 -α and ˆ κ δ, 1 -α , so it follows that ∆ cs ( θ ε ; ˆ P 1 -α ) &lt; δ and ∆ cs ( θ ε ; ˆ P 1 -α ) &lt; δ . Moreover, as the primal solutions for K δ ( θ ε ; P 0 ) and K δ ( θ ε ; P 0 ) are feasible for the relaxed problem whenever P 0 ∈ CS 1 -α P 0 , we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

and similarly ˆ κ δ, 1 -α &gt; κ δ -ε . As ε is arbitrary, we have that κ δ ≥ ˆ κ δ, 1 -α and κ δ ≤ ˆ κ δ, 1 -α holds whenever P 0 ∈ CS 1 -α P 0 . The desired coverage now follows by (30).

- Andrews, I., M. Gentzkow, and J. M. Shapiro (2017). Measuring the sensitivity of parameter estimates to estimation moments. The Quarterly Journal of Economics 132 (4), 1553-1592.
- Andrews, I., M. Gentzkow, and J. M. Shapiro (2020). On the informativeness of descriptive statistics for structural estimates. Econometrica (6), 2231-2258.
- Bonhomme, S. and M. Weidner (2021). Minimizing sensitivity to model misspecification. Quantitative Economics , forthcoming.
- Csisz´ ar, I. and P. C. Shields (2004). Information theory and statistics: A tutorial. Communications and Information Theory 1 (4), 417-528.
- Fang, Z. and A. Santos (2019). Inference on directionally differentiable functions. Review of Economic Studies 86 (1), 377-412.
- Hiriart-Urruty, J.-B. and C. Lemar´ echal (2001). Fundamentals of Convex Analysis . Springer.
- Lehmann, E. L. and G. Casella (1998). Theory of Point Estimation (2 ed.). SpringerVerlag New York, Inc.
- Milgrom, P. and I. Segal (2002). Envelope theorems for arbitrary choice sets. Econo-

metrica 70 (2), 583-601.

- Rockafellar, R. T. (1970). Convex Analysis . Princeton University Press.

## Secondary Online Appendix to 'Counterfactual Sensitivity and Robustness'

## Timothy Christensen Benjamin Connault

(This secondary online appendix is not intended for publication.)

## F Background Material on Orlicz Spaces

In this appendix we briefly review some relevant aspects of the theory of paired Orlicz spaces. We refer the reader to Krasnosel'skii and Rutickii (1961; KR hereafter) for a textbook treatment.

Let L 1 ( F ∗ ) denote the space of (equivalence classes of) all measurable f : U → R with finite first moment under F ∗ . Define

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where ψ ( x ) = φ glyph[star] ( x ) -x with φ glyph[star] denoting the convex conjugate of φ . The space L corresponds to the space L glyph[star] M in KR's notation with M ( x ) = φ (1 + x ) and E corresponds to E N in KR's notation with N ( x ) = ψ ( x ). The condition lim x →∞ xφ ′ ( x ) /φ ( x ) &lt; ∞ in Assumption F (i) implies φ (1+ x ) satisfies KR's ∆ 2 -condition (KR, Theorem 4.1). As such, L and E are separable Banach spaces when equipped with the Orlicz norms

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

respectively (see KR, Section 10 for a discussion of separability and KR, Theorem 10.5 for the norm). Given φ 1 , φ 2 satisfying Assumption F (i), write φ 1 ≺ φ 2 if there exist positive constants c and x 0 such that φ 1 ( x ) ≤ φ 2 ( cx ) for all x ≥ x 0 . If φ 1 ≺ φ 2 and φ 2 ≺ φ 1 then φ 1 and φ 2 are said to be equivalent . Equivalent φ functions induce equivalent spaces L and E and equivalent Orlicz norms on these spaces (KR, Section 13). For example, the functions inducing hybrid and χ 2 divergence are equivalent, and their spaces L and E are equivalent to L 2 ( F ∗ ), and the Orlicz norms ‖ · ‖ φ and ‖ · ‖ ψ are equivalent to the L 2 ( F ∗ ) norm. Similarly, any φ that is equivalent to x p ( p &gt; 1) induces a space L equivalent to L p ( F ∗ ), an Orlicz norm ‖ · ‖ φ equivalent to the L p ( F ∗ ) norm, a space E equivalent to L q ( F ∗ ) with 1 p + 1 q = 1, and a norm ‖ · ‖ ψ equivalent to the L q ( F ∗ ) norm. As with L p spaces, there is a version of H¨ older's inequality, namely

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

which holds for each f ∈ L and g ∈ E (KR, Theorem 9.3).

The spaces L and E are paired spaces under the map 〈· , ·〉 : L×E → R given by

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

A sequence { f n } ⊂ L is said to be E -weakly convergent if {〈 f n , g 〉} converges for each g ∈ E . The space L is E -weakly complete: any E -weakly convergent sequence { f n } ⊂ L has a unique limit f 0 ∈ L for which

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for each g ∈ E (KR, Theorem 14.4). The space L is also E -weakly compact : every ‖ · ‖ φ -norm bounded sequence in L has an E -weakly convergent subsequence (KR, Theorem 14.4). Any E -weakly continuous linear functional glyph[lscript] on L is representable as glyph[lscript] ( f ) = 〈 f, g 〉 for some g ∈ E (KR, Theorem 14.7). Similarly, { g n } ⊂ E is L -weakly convergent if {〈 f, g n 〉} converges for each f ∈ L . As φ (1 + x ) satisfies the ∆ 2 -condition, every L -weakly continuous linear functional glyph[lscript] ′ on E is representable in the form glyph[lscript] ′ ( g ) = 〈 f, g 〉 for f ∈ L . 28

We close this section by noting three useful results, the first of which is from Komunjer and Ragusa (2016). Let L + = { f ∈ L : f ≥ 0 F ∗ -almost everywhere } .

Lemma F.1 Suppose that Assumption F (i) holds. Then:

- (i) the functional m ↦→ E F ∗ [ φ ( m ( U ))] is l.s.c. on L in the E -weak topology;
- (ii) E F ∗ [ φ ( m ( U ))] ≤ δ implies ‖ m ‖ φ ≤ 2 + φ (2) + δ ;
- (iii) E F ∗ [ φ ( m ( U ))] &lt; ∞ if and only if m ∈ L + .

Proof of Lemma F.1. Part (i) is stated on p. 961 of Komunjer and Ragusa (2016). Part (ii) follows by taking c = 1 2 in the definition of ‖ · ‖ φ . For part (iii), it suffices by part (ii) and the fact that φ ( x ) = + ∞ for x &lt; 0 to show E F ∗ [ φ ( m ( U ))] &lt; ∞ for all m ∈ L + . As φ satisfies the ∆ 2 -condition under Assumption F (i), m ∈ L implies E F ∗ [ φ (1 + c | m ( U ) | )] &lt; ∞ for all c &gt; 0. As L contains constant functions and is closed under addition, for any m ∈ L + we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

which, by non-negativity of φ , implies that E F ∗ [ φ ( m ( U ))1 l { m ( U ) ≥ 1 } ] is finite. Finiteness of the remaining term E F ∗ [ φ ( m ( U ))1 l { m ( U ) ≤ 1 } ] follows because max x ∈ [0 , 1] φ ( x ) = φ (0) &lt; ∞ under Assumption F (i).

28 As φ (1 + x ) satisfies the ∆ 2 -condition, in KR's notation L = L glyph[star] M = E M with M ( x ) = φ (1 + x ). Therefore, L -weak convergence corresponds to what KR calls E M -weak convergence on E = E N ⊆ L glyph[star] N with N ( x ) = ψ ( x ).

## G Supplementary Results and Proofs

## G.1 Notation

Throughout this Appendix, we let K δ ( θ ; γ, P ) and K δ ( θ ; γ, P ) denote the criterion functions (11) and (12). We use the notation K glyph[star] δ ( θ ; γ, P ) and K glyph[star] δ ( θ ; γ, P ) to denote their dual forms defined below in (A.2) and (A.3). Similarly, we let ∆( θ ; γ, P ) to denote the primal form of the minimum divergence problem (15) and ∆ glyph[star] ( θ ; γ, P ) to denote its dual form in (16).

For x ∈ R n and A,B ⊂ R n we let d ( x, A ) = inf a ∈ A ‖ x -a ‖ and glyph[vector] d H ( A,B ) = sup a ∈ A d ( a, B ). Let B ε denote a Euclidean ball centered at the origin with radius ε , where the dimension should be obvious from the context. Let T ⊆ R n be a nonempty, closed convex cone with nonempty interior. Let ∂A = cl( A ) \ int( A ) denote the boundary of A ⊂ T (relative to R n ) and ∂ T A = cl( ∂A ∩ int( T )) denote the boundary of A relative to T . For example, if T = R + × R , and A = { ( x, y ) ∈ T : x 2 + y 2 ≤ 1 } , then ∂A = { ( x, y ) ∈ T : x 2 + y 2 = 1 } ∪ { 0 } × [ -1 , 1] and ∂ T A = { ( x, y ) ∈ T : x 2 + y 2 = 1 } .

where ( ηφ ) glyph[star] is the convex conjugate of x ↦→ η · φ ( x ). In particular, ( ηφ ) glyph[star] ( x ) = ηφ glyph[star] ( x/η ) when η &gt; 0. Let Ξ δ ( θ ; γ, P ) and Ξ δ ( θ ; γ, P ) denote the (possibly empty) set of solutions to the dual problems (A.2) and (A.3), respectively. The main result we prove in this subsection is the following:

Proposition G.1 Suppose that Assumption F holds. Then:

- (i) K δ ( θ ; γ, P ) = K glyph[star] δ ( θ ; γ, P ) and K δ ( θ ; γ, P ) = K glyph[star] δ ( θ ; γ, P ) (i.e., strong duality holds);
- (ii) Optimizing over ( η, ζ, λ ) ∈ (0 , ∞ ) × R × Λ yields the same value for K glyph[star] δ ( θ ; γ, P ) and K glyph[star] δ ( θ ; γ, P ) as optimizing over ( η, ζ, λ ) ∈ R + × R × Λ ;
- (iii) If Condition S holds at ( θ, γ, P ) and there is F with D φ ( F ‖ F ∗ ) &lt; δ such that (1) holds under F at ( θ, γ, P ) , then Ξ δ ( θ ; γ, P ) and Ξ δ ( θ ; γ, P ) are nonempty and convex;
- (iv) If Condition S is replaced by Condition S' in (iii), then Ξ δ ( θ ; γ, P ) and Ξ δ ( θ ; γ, P ) are also compact.

We first present some preliminary results used to derive the dual problems and verify the constraint qualification conditions. We derive the dual of (11); the derivation of the dual of (12) follows similarly, replacing k with -k . Fix any θ ∈ Θ and γ ∈ Γ. We drop dependence of k ( u, θ, γ ) and g ( u, θ, γ ) on ( θ, γ ) to simplify notation.

We first establish some facts about ϕ and v . A convex function f : X → R ∪ { + ∞} is proper if f ( x ) &gt; -∞ for all x ∈ X and f ( x ) &lt; + ∞ for some x ∈ X .

Lemma G.1 Suppose that Assumption F holds. Then ϕ is proper and convex.

Proof of Lemma G.1. First note that |〈 m,k 〉| &lt; + ∞ for any m ∈ L by H¨ older's inequality (see (A.1)) and Assumption F (ii). It follows that ϕ ( m,y ) &gt; -∞ for all m ∈ L and y ∈ R d +2 . Take any m ∈ L + . Then Q φ ( m ) &lt; + ∞ by Lemma F.1(iii). Setting y 1 = δ -Q φ ( m ), y 2 =

1 -〈 m, 1 〉 and y 3 = glyph[vector] P -〈 m,g 〉 ensures I C ( Q φ ( m ) -δ + y 1 , 〈 m, 1 〉-1+ y 2 , 〈 m,g 〉-glyph[vector] P + y 3 ) = 0, hence ϕ ( m,y ) &lt; + ∞ . Therefore, ϕ is proper. Convexity of ϕ follows from convexity of m ↦→ Q φ ( m ) and convexity of dom I C ≡ R -×{ 0 } × R d 1 -×{ 0 d 2 } × R d 3 -×{ 0 d 4 } .

Recall C = R d 1 + ×{ 0 d 2 } × R d 3 + ×{ 0 d 4 } . For A,B ⊂ R n , let A -B = { a -b : a ∈ A,b ∈ B } . Let

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The effective domain of f is dom f = { x ∈ X : f ( x ) &lt; + ∞} .

Lemma G.2 Suppose that Assumption F holds. Then:

- (i) v is proper, convex, and l.s.c. on R d +2 with dom v = Y ;
- (ii) A solution to the primal problem (P y ) exists for each y ∈ Y .

Proof of Lemma G.2. Convexity of v follows from Lemma G.1 and Bonnans and Shapiro (2000, Proposition 2.143). Note that Y is the set of all y = ( y 1 , y 2 , y 3 ) ∈ R × R × R d for which there exists m y ∈ L such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Take y ∈ Y and any such m y ∈ L . Then ϕ ( m y , y ) = 〈 m y , k 〉 &lt; + ∞ by the proof of Lemma G.1, and so v ( y ) &lt; + ∞ . Conversely, if y glyph[negationslash]∈ Y then ϕ ( m,y ) = + ∞ for all m ∈ L and so v ( y ) = + ∞ . Therefore, dom v = Y .

To see v is proper, take any y ∈ dom v and let y 1 denote its first element. Then

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the first inequality is by definition of v ( y ) and the second is by inequality (A.1) and Lemma F.1(ii).

Before proving l.s.c. we first prove assertion (ii). Take any y ∈ Y . Choose { m n } ⊂ L such that ϕ ( m n , y ) ↓ v ( y ) as n → ∞ . As Q φ ( m n ) ≤ δ -y 1 holds for each n , { m n } is ‖ · ‖ φ -norm bounded (by Lemma F.1(ii)) and therefore has a E -weakly convergent subsequence { m n l } (see Appendix F). Let m 0 ∈ L denote the E -weak limit. Under Assumption F , we have both lim l →∞ 〈 m n l , 1 〉 = 〈 m 0 , 1 〉 and lim l →∞ 〈 m n l , g 〉 = 〈 m 0 , g 〉 by E -weak convergence. We also have δ -y 1 ≥ lim inf l →∞ Q ( m n l ) ≥ Q ( m 0 ) by Lemma F.1(i). It follows by closedness of dom I C that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Moreover, by E -weak convergence we also have that v ( y ) = lim l →∞ 〈 m n l , k 〉 = 〈 m 0 , k 〉 . Therefore, m 0 solves the primal problem (P y ).

To prove l.s.c., we first show that Y is closed. Take y ∈ cl( Y ). Choose any sequence { y n } ⊂ Y converging to y . Note { v ( y n ) } is bounded by the argument used above to establish properness. Choose a subsequence { n l } for which lim l →∞ v ( y n l ) = liminf n →∞ v ( y n ). By part (ii), there exists a solution m n l to the primal problem for each y n l . The sequence { m n l } is ‖ · ‖ φ -norm bounded (by Lemma F.1(ii)) and hence, taking a further subsequence if necessary, has an E -weak limit m 0 ∈ L . By similar arguments to the above, we may deduce that (A.5) holds for this m 0 at y . Therefore, y ∈ Y (establishing closedness) and v ( y ) &lt; ∞ .

To complete the proof of l.s.c., take any y ∈ Y . Choose any sequence { y n } ⊂ Y converging to y (it is without loss of generality to consider sequences of elements of Y , since v (˜ y ) = + ∞ for ˜ y glyph[negationslash]∈ Y ). By the argument just used to establish closedness, we may extract a subsequence { n l } for which lim l →∞ v ( y n l ) = lim inf n →∞ v ( y n ). Let m n l solve the primal problem along this subsequence. Taking a further subsequence if necessary, { m n l } has a E -weak limit m 0 ∈ L . By similar arguments to the above, we may deduce that (A.5) holds for this m 0 at y . Therefore, by E -weak convergence we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

establishing l.s.c. of v at any y ∈ cl( Y ) ≡ Y . Finally, l.s.c. of v on R d +2 now follows from l.s.c. of v at any y ∈ Y and the fact that Y ≡ dom v is closed.

Lemma G.3 Suppose that Assumption F holds. Then:

- (i) K δ ( θ ; γ, P ) = K glyph[star] δ ( θ ; γ, P ) ;
- (ii) If 0 ∈ ri( Y ) , then the set of dual solutions Ξ δ ( θ ; γ, P ) is nonempty and convex;
- (iii) If 0 ∈ int( Y ) , then Ξ δ ( θ ; γ, P ) is also compact.

Proof of Lemma G.3. For part (i), we need to show that v (0) = v glyph[star]glyph[star] (0). As ϕ is convex by Lemma G.1 and v is proper by Lemma G.2, it follows by Bonnans and Shapiro (2000, Theorem 2.144) that v glyph[star]glyph[star] (0) = min { v (0) , lim inf y → 0 v ( y ) } . But lim inf y → 0 v ( y ) ≥ v (0) by Lemma G.2, and so v (0) = v glyph[star]glyph[star] (0), as required.

For part (ii), non-emptiness of the set of dual solutions follows by Bonnans and Shapiro (2000, Propositions 2.147 and 2.148(iii)), noting that v is convex by Lemma G.2 and v (0) is finite because v is proper by Lemma G.2 and 0 ∈ dom v ≡ Y by assumption. Convexity of the set of dual solutions follows by noting that, in view of (D y ) and (A.6), the dual objective is the pointwise infimum of affine functions of ( η, ζ, λ ), and is therefore concave and u.s.c. Finally, part (iii) follows from Bonnans and Shapiro (2000, Theorem 2.151 and Proposition 2.152).

Now suppose Condition S' holds. Part (i) holds with 'interior' by definition of Condition S'. For part (ii) with 'interior', it suffices to show that Y 2 has positive volume, in which case its relative interior and interior coincide and the result follows by part (ii) above. A sufficient condition is that the functions in g and a function that is constant F ∗ -a.e. are not collinear F ∗ -a.e. We prove this by contradiction. Suppose Condition S' holds but that there exists 0 = λ ∈ R d and ζ ∈ R such that λ ′ ( g ( u ) -glyph[vector] P ) = ζ F ∗ -a.e. Then by Condition S', we have { E F [ g ( U )] -glyph[vector] P : D φ ( F ‖ F ∗ ) &lt; ∞} contains a ε -ball with center c 0 for some c 0 ∈ C and ε &gt; 0. Then for any unit vector x , we have ζ = λ ′ c 0 + ελ ′ x , a contradiction. Thus, part (ii) must hold with 'interior' when Condition S' holds. For part (iii), note that Y ⊇ ( -∞ , δ ] ×Y 2 . Therefore, Y has positive volume as Y 2 as positive volume, so its relative interior and interior coincide and part (iii) with 'interior' follows similarly.

Proof of Proposition G.1. We prove the result for K glyph[star] δ and Ξ δ ; the result for K glyph[star] δ and Ξ δ follows similarly.

Part (i) follows by Lemma G.3(i). For part (ii), in view of (D y ) and (A.6), the dual objective function, say glyph[lscript] ( η, ζ, λ ), is the pointwise infimum of affine functions of ( η, ζ, λ ), and is therefore concave and u.s.c. If glyph[lscript] (0 , ζ, λ ) = -∞ for all ζ ∈ R and λ ∈ Λ, then restricting ( η, ζ, λ ) to (0 , ∞ ) × R × Λ will not affect the dual value. Now suppose glyph[lscript] (0 , ζ, λ ) &gt; -∞ for some ( ζ, λ ) ∈ R × Λ. By u.s.c. of glyph[lscript] ( · , ζ, λ ), we have glyph[lscript] (0 , ζ, λ ) ≥ lim η ↓ 0 glyph[lscript] ( η, ζ, λ ). To prove the reverse

inequality, note by concavity of glyph[lscript] that for any τ ∈ [0 , 1] and ¯ η &gt; 0, we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

because | glyph[lscript] ( η, ζ, λ ) | &lt; + ∞ on (0 , ∞ ) × R × Λ. Therefore lim η ↓ 0 glyph[lscript] ( η, ζ, λ ) ≥ glyph[lscript] (0 , ζ, λ ).

For part (iii), if Condition S holds and there exists F with D φ ( F ‖ F ∗ ) &lt; δ under which (1) holds, then 0 ∈ ri( Y ) by Lemma G.4. Existence and convexity of the set of dual solutions follows by Lemma G.3(ii). Finally, compactness of Ξ δ ( θ ; γ, P ) under Condition S' follows similarly by Lemma G.3(iii) and Lemma G.4, proving part (iv).

## G.3 Additional Details on the Minimum-Divergence Problem

Recall that ∆( θ ; γ, P ) denotes the value of the primal form of the minimum divergence problem (15) and ∆ glyph[star] ( θ ; γ, P ) denotes its dual value in (16). The main result we prove in this subsection is the following:

Proposition G.2 Suppose that Assumption F holds. Then:

- (i) ∆( θ ; γ, P ) = ∆ glyph[star] ( θ ; γ, P ) (i.e., strong duality holds);
- (ii) If Condition S holds at ( θ, γ, P ) , then the set of dual solutions is nonempty and convex;
- (iii) If Condition S' holds at ( θ, γ, P ) , then the set of dual solutions is compact.

We first present some preliminary results. We again drop dependence of k and g on ( θ, γ ) to simplify notation. Define ϕ 2 : L× R d +1 → R ∪ { + ∞} by

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where y = ( y 1 , y 2 ) ∈ R × R d and I C 2 : R d +1 → R ∪ { + ∞} is given by

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The primal problem for y ∈ R d +1 is and its value is

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

In particular, v 2 (0) = ∆( θ, γ, P ). Recall the set Y 2 defined in (A.7)

Lemma G.5 Suppose that Assumption F holds. Then:

- (i) ϕ 2 is proper and convex;

- (ii) v 2 is proper, convex, and dom v 2 = Y 2 ;
- (iii) A solution to the primal problem (P ′ y ) exists and is unique for each y ∈ Y 2 .

Proof of Lemma G.5. Properness and convexity of ϕ 2 and v 2 follow by similar arguments to Lemma G.1 and G.2, and dom v 2 = Y 2 follows by similar arguments to Lemma G.2.

For part (iii), take any y ∈ Y 2 . Choose { m n } ⊂ L such that ϕ 2 ( m n , y ) ↓ v 2 ( y ) as n →∞ . Without loss of generality we may assume ϕ 2 ( m n , y ) &lt; ∞ for each n . Then as ϕ 2 ( m n , y ) = Q φ ( m n ), the sequence { m n } is ‖· ‖ φ -norm bounded (by Lemma F.1(ii)) and therefore has a E -weakly convergent subsequence { m n l } (see Appendix F). Let m 0 ∈ L denote the E -weak limit. We have both lim l →∞ 〈 m n l , 1 〉 = 〈 m 0 , 1 〉 and lim l →∞ 〈 m n l , g 〉 = 〈 m 0 , g 〉 by E -weak convergence. Therefore,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

and so ϕ 2 ( m 0 , y ) = Q φ ( m 0 ). It follows by Lemma F.1(i) that v 2 ( y ) = lim l →∞ Q φ ( m n l ) ≥ Q φ ( m 0 ) ≥ v 2 ( y ), where the final inequality is by definition of v 2 ( y ). Uniqueness of the primal solution follows by strict convexity of φ .

By similar arguments to Appendix G.2, the dual problem of (P ′ y ) is

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The value of (D ′ y ) is obtained by replacing the max with a sup and corresponds to the biconjugate v glyph[star]glyph[star] 2 ( y ) of v 2 ( y ). Hence, v 2 (0) = ∆( θ ; γ, P ) and v glyph[star]glyph[star] 2 (0) = ∆ glyph[star] ( θ ; γ, P ).

Proof of Proposition G.2. For part (i), we need to show v 2 (0) = v glyph[star]glyph[star] 2 (0). As ϕ 2 is convex by Lemma G.5 and v 2 ( y ) ≥ 0 for all y ∈ R d +1 , it follows by Bonnans and Shapiro (2000, Theorem 2.144) that v glyph[star]glyph[star] 2 (0) = min { v 2 (0) , lim inf y → 0 v 2 ( y ) } . Therefore, it suffices to show that v 2 ( y ) is l.s.c. at y = 0.

To do so, let { y n } be any sequence converging to 0. It is without loss of generality to assume lim inf n →∞ v 2 ( y n ) &lt; + ∞ (otherwise there is nothing to prove). Extract a subsequence { n l } along which v 2 ( y n l ) &lt; + ∞ and lim l →∞ v 2 ( y n l ) = lim inf n →∞ v 2 ( y n ). The primal problem at y n l has a solution m n l for each l by Lemma G.5. As v 2 ( y n l ) = Q φ ( m n l ) ≤ C for all l and a finite positive constant C , the sequence { m n l } is ‖ · ‖ φ -norm bounded (by Lemma F.1(ii)). Extracting a further subsequence if necessary, we may assume the sequence { m n l } is E -weakly convergent to some m 0 ∈ L . By similar arguments to the proof of Lemma G.5, we may deduce that (A.10) holds for this m 0 at y = 0. Then by Lemma F.1(i), we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

as required. Note by way of contradiction that the preceding argument also implies that lim inf n →∞ v 2 ( y n ) = + ∞ for all sequences y n → 0 whenever v 2 (0) = + ∞ .

For parts (ii) and (iii), Lemma G.4 shows that Conditions S and S' are sufficient for 0 ∈ ri( Y 2 ) and 0 ∈ int( Y 2 ), respectively. Parts (ii) and (iii) now follow by similar arguments to Lemma G.3(ii)(iii).

## G.4 Stability of Constraint Qualifications under Perturbations

Lemma G.6 Suppose that Assumption F holds and Condition S' holds at ( θ, γ, P ) . Then there exists a neighborhood N of P such that Condition S' holds at ( θ, γ, ˜ P ) for each ˜ P ∈ N .

Proof of Lemma G.6. Recall glyph[vector] P = ( P, 0 d 3 + d 4 ) and let glyph[vector] ˜ P = ( ˜ P, 0 d 3 + d 4 ). By Condition S', there exists ε &gt; 0 such that B 2 ε ⊆ ( { E F [ g ( U, θ, γ )] -glyph[vector] P : F ∈ N ∞ } + C ) . Then for any ˜ P for which ‖ P -˜ P ‖ &lt; ε , we have ‖ ( E F [ g ( U, θ, γ )] -glyph[vector] P ) -( E F [ g ( U, θ, γ )] -glyph[vector] ˜ P ) ‖ &lt; ε for all F ∈ N ∞ . Hence, B ε ⊆ ( { E F [ g ( U, θ, γ )] -glyph[vector] ˜ P : F ∈ N ∞ } + C ).

Lemma G.7 Suppose that Assumption F holds, each entry of g is E -continuous in ( θ, γ ) , and Condition S' holds at ( θ, γ, P ) . Then there exists a neighborhood N of ( θ, γ, P ) such that Condition S' holds at ( ˜ θ, ˜ γ, ˜ P ) for each ( ˜ θ, ˜ γ, ˜ P ) ∈ N .

As Condition S' holds at ( θ, γ, P ), there exists sufficiently large δ such that 0 ∈ int( { E F [ g ( U, θ, γ )] -glyph[vector] P : F ∈ N δ } + C ). Therefore, we may choose ε &gt; 0 such that B 4 ε ⊆ int( { E F [ g ( U, θ, γ )] -glyph[vector] P : F ∈ N δ } + C ).

Let M δ = { d F d F ∗ : F ∈ N δ } . Then M δ is a ‖· ‖ φ -bounded subset of L by Lemma F.1(ii). By E -continuity, there exists a neighborhood N 1 of ( θ, γ ) such that for any ( ˜ θ, ˜ γ ) ∈ N 1 and with r denoting any entry of g 1 , . . . , g 4 , we have

## G.6 Supplemental Results on Directional Derivatives

This Appendix presents results to supplement the proof of Theorem 6.3.

Lemma G.10 Let Θ δ,a = { θ ∈ Θ δ ( P 0 ) : K δ ( θ ; P 0 ) ≤ b δ ( P 0 ) + a } . Suppose that Assumptions F and M(i)-(iii),(v),(vi) hold. Then there is a &gt; 0 and a neighborhood N of P 0 such that Condition S' holds at ( θ, P ) and ∆( θ ; P ) &lt; δ for all ( θ, P ) ∈ Θ δ,a × N .

Proof of Lemma G.10. Suppose the assertion is false. Then there is a n ↓ 0, θ n ∈ Θ δ,a n , and P n → P for which Condition S' does not hold at ( θ n ; P n ) and/or ∆( θ n ; P n ) ≥ δ . By Assumption M(v), we can extract a convergent subsequence θ n l → θ . By similar arguments to Step 1 of the proof of Theorem 6.2, we may deduce that θ ∈ Θ δ . Then by Lemma E.5 and Assumption M(iii)(vi) we must have that Condition S' holds at ( θ n l , P b l ) and ∆( θ n l ; P n l ) &lt; δ for all l sufficiently large, a contradiction.

Lemma G.11 Suppose that the conditions of Theorem 6.3 hold. Then:

- (i) | ̂ db δ,P 0 [ h 1 ] -̂ db δ,P 0 [ h 2 ] | ≤ C n ‖ h 1 -h 2 ‖ and | ̂ db δ,P 0 [ h 1 ] -̂ db δ,P 0 [ h 2 ] | ≤ C n ‖ h 1 -h 2 ‖ for all h 1 , h 2 ∈ R d 1 + d 2 with C n = O p (1) and C n = O p (1) ;
- (ii) ̂ db δ,P 0 [ h ] → p db δ,P 0 [ h ] and ̂ db δ,P 0 [ h ] → p db δ,P 0 [ h ] for all h ∈ R d 1 + d 2 .

Proof of Lemma G.11. We prove the results for the lower values; the results for the upper values follow similarly.

Step 1: Recall Θ δ,a from Lemma G.10. We show there exists a sequence a n ↓ 0 such that Θ δ ⊆ ˆ Θ δ,n ⊆ Θ δ,a n holds with probability approaching one (wpa1).

Fix any sufficiently small a &gt; 0 and let N be the neighborhood from Lemma G.10. As Θ δ ⊂ Θ δ,a , for all ( θ, P ) ∈ Θ δ × N , we have that ∆( θ ; P ) &lt; δ and Condition S' holds at ( θ, P ). Therefore, Θ δ ⊆ Θ δ ( ˆ P ) wpa1 by consistency of ˆ P . Moreover, Λ δ ( θ ; P ) is nonempty and

compact for all ( θ, P ) ∈ Θ δ × N (cf. Proposition G.1(iv)). By similar arguments to the proof of Theorem 6.2, we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for any ( θ, P ) ∈ Θ δ × N , and so

As Θ δ is compact (see Step 1 of the proof of Theorem 6.2) and ( θ, P ) ↦→ max λ 12 ∈ Λ δ ( θ ; P ) ‖ λ 12 ‖ is u.s.c. (by Lemma G.9(ii)) on Θ δ × N , we may choose a neighborhood N ′ of P 0 upon which sup θ ∈ Θ δ max λ 12 ∈ Λ δ ( θ ; P ) ‖ λ 12 ‖ &lt; ∞ . Setting P = ˆ P in (A.20) and noting b δ ( P 0 ) -b δ ( ˆ P ) = O p ( n -1 / 2 ) by Theorem 6.2, we may deduce that sup θ ∈ Θ δ K δ ( θ ; ˆ P ) -b δ ( ˆ P ) ≤ O p ( n -1 / 2 ) and therefore that Θ δ ⊆ ˆ Θ δ,n wpa1.

By the almost sure representation theorem (see, e.g., Shapiro (1991, Theorem A1)), there exists a sequence of random vectors { ( Z n , ˆ ν n ) } and a random vector Z defined on a probability space (Ω , F , P ) with Z n = d √ n ( ˆ P -P 0 ), ˆ ν n = d ˆ ν , Z ∼ N (0 , Σ), and ( Z n , ˆ ν n ) → a.s. ( Z, ν ). Let P n = P 0 + n -1 / 2 Z n so that P n = d ˆ P . Fix any ω ∈ Ω for which ( Z n ( ω ) , ˆ ν n ( ω )) → ( Z ( ω ) , ν ( ω )). Let ˆ Θ δ,n ( ω ) = { θ ∈ Θ δ ( P n ( ω )) : K δ ( θ ; P n ( ω )) ≤ b δ ( P n ( ω )) + ν ( ω ) √ log n/n } . The set Θ δ ( P n ( ω )), and therefore ˆ Θ δ,n ( ω ), is nonempty for n sufficiently large.

Suppose there is { θ n l ( ω ) } with θ n l ( ω ) ∈ ˆ Θ δ,n ( ω ) \ Θ δ,a for all l . By Assumption M(v) (taking a further subsequence if necessary) we have θ n l ( ω ) → θ ( ω ) ∈ Θ. We may deduce by similar arguments to Step 1 in the proof of Theorem 6.2 that θ ( ω ) ∈ Θ δ . Then by Lemma G.9(i) and Assumption M(iii)(vi), we have lim l →∞ K δ ( θ n l ( ω ); P 0 ) = K δ ( θ ( ω ); P 0 ) = b δ ( P 0 ). Moreover, lim l →∞ ∆( θ n l ( ω ); P 0 ) = ∆( θ ( ω ); P 0 ) &lt; δ by Lemma G.8 and Assumption M(iii)(vi). Therefore, θ n l ( ω ) ∈ Θ δ,a for l sufficiently large, a contradiction. Therefore, ˆ Θ δ,n ⊆ Θ δ,a wpa1 for each a &gt; 0.

In view of the above, we may choose a n ↓ 0 so that ˆ Θ δ,n ⊆ Θ δ,a n wpa1.

Step 2: We prove part (i). Let a &gt; 0 and the neighborhood N be as in Lemma G.10. It follows by Proposition G.1(iv) that Λ δ ( θ ; P ) is compact and nonempty for all ( θ, P ) ∈ Θ δ,a × N . ## G.7 Proofs for Appendix A

Proof of Proposition A.1. We prove only the result for K Π δ ; the result for K Π δ follows similarly.

Dropping dependence of k and g on ( θ, γ ) to simplify notation, we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where the first line uses Π-invariance of F ∈ N Π δ and the second is because N Π δ ⊆ N δ . Problem (A.22) is an optimization over N δ may therefore be restated as problem (34) by virtue of Proposition 2.1. The right-hand side of (34) is therefore a lower bound for K Π δ ( θ ; γ, P ).

To establish equality, first suppose problem (A.22) is infeasible. Then problem (33) must also be infeasible because N δ ⊇ N Π δ , so the value of problem (33) is + ∞ . By Proposition 2.1, the value of the dual program to (A.22), and hence the right-hand side of (34), must also be + ∞ .

Now suppose that problem (A.22) is feasible. We claim that there exists a minimizing F ∈ N δ that is Π-invariant, so it is without loss of generality to optimize over N δ rather than N Π δ . First note by Lemma G.2 there exists a (not necessarily Π-invariant) minimizing F ∈ N δ , say F 0 . Let m 0 = d F 0 d F ∗ and let κ 0 = E F 0 [ k Π ( U )] denote the minimizing value of (A.22). Note that κ 0 must also be the value of the dual program (34) by virtue of Proposition 2.1. Define m Π ( u ) = 1 | Π | ∑ glyph[pi1] ∈ Π m 0 ( glyph[pi1] u ) and F Π by d F Π = m Π d F ∗ .

Wehave E F ∗ [ m Π ( U )] = 1 | Π | ∑ glyph[pi1] ∈ Π E F ∗ [ m 0 ( glyph[pi1] U )] = 1 | Π | ∑ glyph[pi1] ∈ Π E F ∗ [ m 0 ( U )] = 1 by Π-invariance of F ∗ , so F Π is a probability measure. By convexity of φ we also have

because F 0 ∈ N δ . This proves F Π ∈ N δ . Finally, as Π is a group, for any function h and any glyph[pi1] ∈ Π, we have

Therefore, m Π ( u ) = m Π ( glyph[pi1] u ) for each glyph[pi1] ∈ Π. It follows that for any glyph[pi1] ∈ Π and A ⊂ U ,

Proof of Proposition A.2. The minimization problem is additively separable across each x ∈ X . The result follows by applying Proposition 2.1 for each x .

Proof of Proposition A.3. Follows by similar arguments to the proof of Proposition 2.1.

## G.8 Proofs for Appendix B

Proof of Lemma B.1. We prove the result only for K ∞ ; the result for K ∞ follows similarly. First suppose that inf K ∞ is finite. Fix any ε &gt; 0. Then there is F ε ∈ N ∞ and θ ε ∈ Θ such that (1) holds at ( θ ε , γ 0 , P 0 ) under F ε and E F ε [ k ( U, θ ε , γ 0 )] &lt; inf K ∞ + ε . Then for any δ ≥ D φ ( F ε ‖ F 0 ) we have κ δ &lt; inf K ∞ + ε . Conversely, inf K ∞ = -∞ , then for each n ∈ N there exists F n ∈ N ∞ and θ n ∈ Θ such that (1) holds at ( θ n , γ 0 , P 0 ) under F n and E F n [ k ( U, θ n , γ 0 )] &lt; -n . But then for any δ ≥ D φ ( F n ‖ F 0 ) we necessarily have κ δ &lt; -n .

Lemma B.3 is proved by modifying results of Csisz´ ar and Mat´ uˇ s (2012) from a setting with moment equality restrictions to one with moment inequality restrictions.

## H Additional Details for Example 2.4

We first check Condition S'. Note D φ ( F ‖ F ∗ ) = 1 2 θ 2 with F the N ( θ, 1) distribution. Therefore, | θ | &lt; √ 2 δ implies that Condition S' holds. In what follows we implicitly assume | θ | &lt; √ 2 δ . By Proposition 2.1 and the discussion thereafter for KL neighborhoods,

It remains to derive an asymptotic expansion for the optimal value κ δ . To this end, by the envelope theorem and the FOC for λ , we have

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where η &gt; 0 solves the inner problem at θ . It follows that K δ ( θ ) is maximized at θ = 0 for all δ &gt; 0, and therefore that κ δ = 1 -2 πe -2 δ -1 (1 + o (1)).

## References

Bickel, P. J., C. A. Klaassen, Y. Ritov, and J. A. Wellner (1993). Efficient and Adaptive Estimation for Semiparametric Models . Springer-Verlag, New York.

Bonnans, J. and A. Shapiro (2000). Perturbation Analysis of Optimization Problems . Springer.

- Csisz´ ar, I. and F. Mat´ uˇ s (2012). Generalized minimizers of convex integral functionals, Bregman distance, Pythagorean identities. Kybernetika 48 (4), 637-689.
- Gradshteyn, I. S. and I. M. Ryzhik (2014). Table of Integrals, Series, and Products (8 ed.). Academic Press.
- Komunjer, I. and G. Ragusa (2016). Existence and characterization of conditional density projections. Econometric Theory 32 (4), 947-987.
- Krasnosel'skii, M. A. and Ya. B. Rutickii (1961). Convex Functions and Orlicz Spaces . P. Noordhoff Ltd., Groningen.
- Newey, W. K. and D. McFadden (1994). Chapter 36 large sample estimation and hypothesis testing. Volume 4 of Handbook of Econometrics , pp. 2111-2245. Elsevier.
- Rockafellar, R. T. (1970). Convex Analysis . Princeton University Press.
- Rockafellar, R. T. and R. J. B. Wets (1998). Variational Analysis . Springer.
- Shapiro, A. (1991). Asymptotic analysis of stochastic programs. Annals of Operations Research 30 (1), 169-186.
